questions.question: Explain the difference between a strong entity and a weak entity with an example.
keyPoints: Strong entity has its own key attribute, can exist independently
Weak entity depends on strong entity, has partial key
Example: Department (strong) - Employee (weak), as employee needs dept_id

questions.question: What are the different types of attributes in E-R modeling? Give an example for each.
keyPoints: Simple/Single-valued: Cannot be divided, one value (e.g., Age)
Composite: Can be divided (e.g., Address broken into Street, City, Pin)
Multivalued: Multiple values (e.g., Phone_numbers)
Derived: Calculated from other attributes (e.g., Age from DOB)
Key: Uniquely identifies (e.g., Roll_No)

questions.question: Define cardinality in relationships and explain the three types of cardinalities.
keyPoints: Cardinality indicates number of instances of one entity associated with another
One-to-One (1:1): Each entity in A relates to exactly one in B (e.g., Person-Passport)
One-to-Many (1:N): One A relates to many B (e.g., Department-Employees)
Many-to-Many (M:N): Many A relate to many B (e.g., Student-Courses)

questions.question: Differentiate between total participation and partial participation with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Total participation: Every entity MUST participate in relationship (mandatory)
Partial participation: Entity MAY participate (optional)
Example: In Enrollment, every Student must enroll (total) but a Course may have no students (partial)
Represented by double line (total) and single line (partial) in ERD

questions.question: What is a composite attribute? How is it represented in an E-R diagram? Give an example.
keyPoints: Composite attribute can be broken into smaller sub-attributes
Represented as an oval with lines connecting to sub-attribute ovals
Example: Name can be split into First_Name, Middle_Name, Last_Name; Address into House_No, Street, City, State, PIN

questions.question: Explain the concept of a relationship set and relationship degree with examples.
keyPoints: Relationship set: Collection of relationships of the same type
Degree: Number of entities participating in a relationship
Unary/Recursive: One entity set relates to itself (Employee-Employee: manages)
Binary: Two entity sets (Student-Course)
Ternary: Three entity sets (Student-Course-Professor)

questions.question: Draw an E-R diagram for a hospital management system considering the following: Patients, Doctors, Wards, and Treatments. Define appropriate attributes for each entity and specify relationships with cardinalities and participation constraints.
keyPoints: Entities: Patient (Patient_ID, Name, Age, Gender, Address, Phone), Doctor (Doctor_ID, Name, Specialization, Salary), Ward (Ward_No, Ward_Type, Capacity), Treatment (Treatment_ID, Date, Duration, Cost)
Relationships:
- Doctor treats Patient (1:N - one doctor treats many patients)
- Patient occupies Ward (1:1 or 1:N based on design)
- Doctor prescribes Treatment to Patient (M:N - doctor can prescribe many treatments to many patients)
- Treatment given in Ward (N:1 - many treatments in one ward)
Participations: Patient total in 'Occupies' (must be in some ward), partial in 'Treated_by'
Include weak entity if needed for Treatment

questions.question: Design an E-R diagram for a university database system with entities: Student, Course, Professor, and Department. Include relevant attributes, identify primary keys, define relationships between entities with cardinalities, and specify total/partial participation constraints.
keyPoints: Entities with attributes:
- Student (Stud_ID, Name, DOB, Address, Year) - Key: Stud_ID
- Course (Course_ID, Course_Name, Credits) - Key: Course_ID
- Professor (Prof_ID, Name, Designation, Salary) - Key: Prof_ID
- Department (Dept_ID, Dept_Name, HOD) - Key: Dept_ID
Relationships:
- Student enrolls in Course (M:N, partial both)
- Professor teaches Course (1:N from Prof to Course, partial both)
- Professor belongs to Department (N:1, total for Prof - every professor belongs to dept)
- Course belongs to Department (N:1, total for Course - every course belongs to a department)
- Professor is HOD of Department (1:1, partial both)

questions.question: Explain the Entity-Relationship model proposed by Peter Chen. Discuss its components (entities, attributes, relationships) and how they are represented graphically in E-R diagrams.
keyPoints: Peter Chen proposed E-R model in 1976 as conceptual framework for database design
Entities: Real-world objects that can be identified uniquely, represented by rectangles
Attributes: Properties/characteristics of entities, shown as ovals
- Types: Simple, Composite, Single-valued, Multivalued, Derived, Key
Relationships: Associations between entities, depicted as diamonds
- Can have attributes of their own
- Cardinality: 1:1, 1:N, M:N
- Participation: Total (mandatory) and Partial (optional)
E-R diagram provides blueprint for database structure, bridges real-world requirements and database implementation
